dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 responding for breathing problems on Main Street.
Hello, I'm Jake, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing, and my legs feel really swollen.
Can you tell me your name, and where we are right now?
What is today's date?
Can you briefly describe what happened to cause you to call 911?
When did you start to feel this way today?
It started about three hours ago. My breathing seemed to get worse.
Have you experienced this kind of breathing difficulty before?
Yes, I have COPD and it flares up sometimes, especially when I can't get my fluids off from dialysis.
When was your last dialysis session?
I went on Friday, and I lost about 4 liters.
Have you noticed any swelling in your legs that you think is related?
Yes, both legs are swollen and pitting when I press on them.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate the pain in your legs?
It's a 4 right now and it's mostly dull.
Can you take a deep breath and let me listen to your lungs?
I hear some faint expiratory wheezing on both sides. You're not showing signs of severe difficulty breathing or tachypnea.
Let me check your pulse and skin tone. Pulse is strong and regular.
Patient's pulse is 87 BPM.
Respirations are 18 and mildly labored. You're not using accessory muscles to breathe.
Blood pressure is 88/52.
You don't appear pale or diaphoretic. Is your alertness level different than usual?
I am a little more lethargic than normal and not as alert today.
I can feel mild swelling in your abdomen. Has that been going on?
Yes, it's been getting worse for a few days.
SpO2 is 99% on room air. No signs of hypoxia at this time.
Blood sugar is at 137 mg/dL.
We can increase your oxygen by a bit. Can we do that?
Yes, please. I ran out of my inhaler and Ventolin today.
The EKG shows a sinus rhythm.
Let me take a 12-lead ECG to get a better look at the heart activity.
I'm going to start an IV in your arm, using an 18-gauge needle in the forearm (extremity vein).
We obtained a 4-lead ECG successfully.
Now placing the 12-lead ECG as detailed in protocol.
Have you had any chest pain, headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, fever, cough, or other symptoms besides the swelling and breathing issue?
No chest pain. I do have a history of heart failure, though.

The 12-lead came back, so I'll briefly recap: sinus rhythm with some signs possibly of anterolateral ischemia and minimal evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) with ST-T wave abnormalities (STT abn) in V5.
Based on our assessment, we'll follow the protocol for pulmonary edema and CHF exacerbation.
I am going to retake your vitals now. Let's start with pulse.
Pulse is down to 77 now.
Breathing is still 18 per minute.
Blood pressure improved a little, 110/55.
SpO2 increased to 100%. Have you felt any improvement?
Glucose now reads zero. I'll double check that again.
The IV is still flowing correctly without issues so far.
Is the leg pain still 4, or has it improved?
Still 4. It's not getting worse, but I'm not feeling much better either.
Let me reaffirm if you have any symptoms related to COVID or fever, cough, or any other symptoms?
No fever or cough. Still just some mild leg discomfort.
Where would you prefer to be taken for further treatment?
I want to go to VCU if possible.
I'll help secure you on the stretcher with help from the partner.
Patient is now safely secured, IV secured, EKG leads in good position.
We've arrived at VCU; I'll hand over the paperwork and patient now.
You use Lasix at home. Are you still taking it?
Yes, but I am producing only small amounts of urine.
You also use Symbicort for COPD. When did you last use it?
Today, but it ran out so I can't continue it.
I've noted you have an allergy to Aspirin, Lisinopril, Tramadol, and Ibuprofen. Do you have any reactions today?
No allergic reactions today.
You're fully alert and oriented to all four spheres: person, place, time, and situation (A&Ox4).
You are currently receiving oxygen via nasal cannula set at 5 liters per minute.
You're on the medications Hydralazine and Isosorbide Dinitrate at home. Do you take them regularly?
Yes, I take them as prescribed.
Just to confirm, you also take atorvastatin, clonidine hydrochloride, doxycycline, gabapentin, levofloxacin, metoprolol, Plavix, and sertraline at home, correct?
Yes, that's right.
Prior to transport, we assessed that your airway was clear and patent.

Hospital, we are now transferring a 57-year-old male with CHF and COPD exacerbation along with a medical history of lupus erythematosus, diabetes non-insulin dependent, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery bypass graft, and renal disease.
Completed paperwork with all patient-related details.
Would you say the 4 level is consistent or slightly down?
Slightly better, but not much.
Any new medical issues or complaints since earlier?
No new symptoms, just ongoing leg discomfort and minor breathing.
Will monitor for any sudden changes and communicate.
The 12-lead EKG captured a sinus rhythm with a few supraventricular premature beats. It also showed some possibly signs of anterolateral ischemia.
Patient and gear securely fastened for ride to VCU.
Patient and gear securely fastened for ride to VCU.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with occasional supraventricular premature complexes, ST-T changes in V5 as detected.
You once had a cardiac arrest in the past. Do you agree with that information?
Yes, that's correct.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation (A&Ox4). How does that match with how you're feeling today?
I am oriented, but I am more lethargic than usual.
Your GCS score is 15; you appear more lethargic than normal.
Blood pressure maintains steady as we get to VCU.
